analogy: Think of "Suggestions for Project Work" through the chapter's own opening images: Indian Society 118 This chapter suggests some small practical research projects that you can try out. There is a big difference between reading about research and actually doing it. Practical experience of trying to answer a question and collecting evidence systematically is a very valuable... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 7.1 V ariety of Methods, 7.2 Possible theMes and subjects for sMall, RESEARCH, TOPIC / AREA. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Suggestions for Project Work is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that indian Society 118 This chapter suggests some small practical research projects that you can try out.

The text explains that there is a big difference between reading about research and actually doing it.

The text explains that practical experience of trying to answer a question and collecting evidence systematically is a very valuable experience.

The text explains that this experience will hopefully introduce you to the excitement and also some of the difficulties of sociological research.

The text explains that before you read this chapter, please refer once again to Chapter 5 (“Doing Sociology: Research Methods”) in the Class XI textbook, Introducing Sociology.

The text explains that the projects suggested here have tried to anticipate the potential problems of organising this kind of activity for large number of students in different kinds of schools located in different kinds of contexts.

The text explains that these are intended just to give you a feel for research.

The text explains that a “real” research project would obviously be more elaborate and involve much more time and effort than is possible in your setting.

The text explains that these are meant as suggestions; feel free to think up ideas of your own in consultation with your teachers.

The text explains that every research question needs an appropriate or suitable research method.

The text explains that a given question may be answered with more than one method, but a given research method is not necessarily appropriate for all questions.

The text explains that in other words, for most research questions one has a choice of possible methods but this choice is usually limited.

The text explains that one of the first tasks of the researcher – after carefully specifying the research question – is to select a suitable method.

The text explains that this selection must be done not only according to technical criteria (i.e., the degree of compatibility between question and method), but also practical considerations.

The text explains that these latter might include the amount of time available to do the research; the resources available in terms of both people and materials; the circumstances or situations in which it has to be done, and so on.

The text explains that for example, let us suppose you are interested in comparing co-educational schools with ‘boys only’ or ‘girls only’ schools.

The text explains that you must first formulate a specific question that you want to answer.

The text explains that are children in single sex schools happier than children in co-educational schools, or some other such question.

The text explains that having decided on a specific question, the next step is to choose the appropriate method.

The text explains that for the last question, ‘Are school children in single sex schools happier?’, for example, you could choose to interview students of different kinds of schools.

The text explains that in the interview you could ask them directly how they felt about their school.

The text explains that as an alternative, you could try to use a different method – say that of direct observation – to answer the research question.

The text explains that so, after observing different kinds of schools for sufficient time, you could hope to answer your question.

The text explains that this would involve preparing a questionnaire designed to get information on how students felt about their schools.

The text explains that you would then distribute the questionnaire to an equal number of students in each kind of school.

The text explains that you would then collect the filled-in questionnaires and analyse the results.

The text explains that you must first make enough copies of the questionnaire.

The text explains that next, you may need permission from teachers to distribute the questionnaire to students in their classrooms.

The text explains that after you have distributed the questionnaire you may find that many people have not bothered to return it to you or have not answered all questions, or other such problems.

The text explains that you then have to decide how to deal with this – go back to your respondents and ask them to complete the questionnaires; or ignore the incomplete questionnaires and consider only the complete ones; consider only the completed answers, and so on.

The text explains that 7.1 V ariety of Methods You may remember the discussion of research methods in Chapter 5 of the Class XI textbook, Introducing Sociology.

The text explains that this may be a good time to revisit this chapter and refresh your memory.

The text explains that s urvey Method A survey usually involves asking a relatively large number of people (such as 30, 100, 2000, and so on; what is considered ‘large’ depends on the context and the kind of topic) the same fixed set of questions.

The text explains that the questions may be asked by an investigator in person where they are read out to the respondent, and his/her answers are noted down by the investigator.

The text explains that or the questionnaire may be handed over to the respondents who then fill it up themselves and give it back.

The text explains that the disadvantage is that the questions to be asked are already fixed.

The text explains that so, if a question is misunderstood by the respondents, then wrong or misleading results can be produced.

The text explains that if a respondent says something interesting then this cannot be followed up with further questions on the subject because you have to stick to the questionnaire format.

The text explains that moreover, questionnaires are like a snapshot taken at one particular moment.

The text explains that indian Society 120 IntervIewS An interview is different from a survey in that it is always conducted in person and usually involves much fewer persons (as few as 5, 20, or 40, usually not much more than that).

The text explains that interviews may be structured, that is, follow a pre-determined pattern of questions or unstructured, where only a set of topics is pre-decided, and the actual questions emerge as part of a conversation.

The text explains that interviews have the advantage of being flexible in that promising topics may be pursued in greater detail, questions may be refined or modified along the way, and clarifications may be sought.

The text explains that the disadvantage of the interview method is that it cannot cover a large number of people and is limited to presenting the views of a select group of individuals. obServatIon Observation is a method where the researcher must systematically watch and record what is happening in whatever context or situation that has been chosen for the research.

The text explains that indian Society 126 RESEARCH TOPIC / AREA TYPE OF RESEARCH METHOD / TECHNIQUE OBSERVATION SURVEY Modes of Public Transport; Local Railway or Bus Station Modes of behaviour, expected etiquette, space sharing Opinions on changes over time; experiences, difficulties etc.

The text explains that domestic Appliances (Use of cooking fuel/ mode; fan, cooler, ac; iron; fridge; mixie...) Patterns of use; domestic division of labour; gender aspects Attitudes/memories relating to different type of appliances Use of Public Spaces (roadside, empty land, etc) Observe how comparable open spaces are used in different localities Opinions of a cross- section of people on different uses of specific public spaces Changing Aspirations of School Children at different ages (e.g.

The text explains that classes 5, 8, 11) Not suitable Boys and Girls Adults of different generations (from memory) Place of the means of communication in social life (from mobile phones to satellite TV) Watch how people use mobile phones in public – what place do these devices have in their lives?

The text explains that how much TV do different kind of people watch, and what are their preferred programmes?

The text explains that suggestions for Project Work 127 TYPE OF RESEARCH METHOD / TECHNIQUE ARCHIVAL INTERVIEWS COMMENTS / SUGGESTIONS Newspaper and other sources for history of change Views of regular vs. occasional users; men vs. women, etc.

The text explains that advertisement patterns for different kinds of appliances How do different type of people respond to specific appliances?

The text explains that boys to be encouraged to do this; should not become a ‘girl’s topic’ What were the different uses to which a particular space was put over the years?

The text explains that do people of different social classes, groups have different views on use of space?

The text explains that best to take familiar, specific places that people know about and relate to Depends on availability of material from the past (such as school essays on this subject) Talk to one group about their own evolution; or talk to different age groups Interviewees should not be from own school Analysis of media coverage and content on any current issue of interest What do people feel about the decline of letter writing after the coming of phones?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Indian Society, Chapter 7: Suggestions for Project Work

prelims_traps: Do not treat 7.1 V ariety of Methods and 7.2 Possible theMes and subjects for sMall as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Suggestions for Project Work; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Suggestions for Project Work through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 7.1 V ariety of Methods and 7.2 Possible theMes and subjects for sMall to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
